AuriOS · Sopia — Human from Birth · Physical AI Layer

Sofie Bear
AuriOS AI Edge Device

A full working document for the teddy-bear voice node, the broader IO research path, and the first-morning hardware build sequence.

DocumentWeb Draft v0.1
Primary DeviceSofie Bear
Target StateVoice IO Node
Build RuleBench first, bear last
Immediate morning rule: start with the Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W alone. No ReSpeaker HAT, no PiSugar, no speaker, no case hardware, no bear. Insert the imaged microSD card, power only the Pi through the Pi power input, observe boot behavior, then try to reach sofie-bear.local or its router-assigned IP address. Do not stack hardware until the bare Pi proves it boots.

1. Executive Summary

Sofie Bear is the first concrete AuriOS AI edge device for the Sopia project: a voice-interactive stuffed bear that begins as a practical Raspberry Pi voice assistant and grows into a proper PAIPy node as the AuriOS infrastructure matures. The initial version should be treated as a thin client. The bear captures speech, sends the heavy work to cloud services and AuriOS, then speaks back through a small internal speaker.

The project should not be treated as a toy hack in isolation. The teddy bear is Track 1: a delivery-focused voice node for Sofie. The related Auri Button, Auri Node, smart-key, environmental sensor, presence, and KTM telemetry work all belong under the same heading for now: AuriOS physical IO and AI edge devices. The button is not part of the bear build, but it validates the same thesis from another direction: intelligence lives in AuriOS and MEIL; physical objects become access points.

The recovered evidence shows that the project was specified, the boot disk was imaged, and the hardware path was documented. The runtime application was not yet completed. That is good news: we are not recovering lost code; we are now ready to build it cleanly.

2. The Device Thesis

The core idea is simple: every object in a member’s world can become an AuriOS node if it can sense, identify, speak, listen, display, vibrate, actuate, or authenticate. The teddy bear is the softest and most emotionally meaningful first example. It has a microphone input, a speaker output, a persistent identity, and a project context: Sopia.

In AuriOS terms, Sofie Bear sits primarily in Category 07 and Category 15. Category 07 is voice and audio input: microphones, microphone arrays, voice activity detection, and acoustic capture. Category 15 is speech and language IO: wake word, speech-to-text, turn-taking, text-to-speech, and persistent spoken interaction. The bear also uses Category 02 for audio output through its speaker. In future versions, it can gain haptics, NFC, location, vision, or environmental sensing, but version 1 should stay disciplined.

The device does not need to be “smart” in the heavy-compute sense. The smart part is AuriOS. The device only needs to capture clean audio, maintain a reliable connection, enforce safe output volume, and identify itself as sofie-bear. That division keeps the physical build achievable and gives the software a clean migration path from standalone script to MEIL session logging and eventually to PAIPy device registration.

3. Sofie Bear Under the Larger AuriOS Edge-Device Heading

Sofie Bear

The delivery device. A voice-interactive teddy bear for Sofie using Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W, ReSpeaker 2-Mics HAT V2.0, speaker, and eventually PiSugar 3. Its job is warm spoken interaction and Sopia memory capture.

Track 1Voice IOCat 07 + 15

Auri Button / Smart Key

The wearable trigger and silent IO path. Version 1 can be BLE-only with the phone handling audio. Later versions add mic, speaker, haptic motor, GPS, and NFC. It validates push-to-talk, silent interrupt, and identity-key concepts.

IO ResearchBLEHaptics

Auri Node v1

The room-scale voice terminal. It is less personal than the bear but more general: an always-available home, desk, or office node connected to MEIL. It informs the electronics and acoustic architecture for future physical nodes.

Physical LayerHome NodePAIPy

4. Sofie Bear Architecture

Version 1 should be built as a staged system, not a sealed product. The Raspberry Pi boots first. Then the network path is confirmed. Then the audio HAT is attached and tested. Then the microphone and speaker chain is confirmed. Only after the electronics and software have passed bench tests should the assembly move into a teddy bear enclosure.

Wake / TriggerPorcupine, button, or manual test
Mic CaptureReSpeaker 2-Mics HAT
STTWhisper / cloud STT
ContextSopia prompt, later MEIL/MQI
LLMAnthropic Claude / Auri
TTSElevenLabs / other voice
OutputJST speaker in bear chest

At first, the Sopia context can live in a local text file or hardcoded system prompt. That is acceptable for the Christmas version. The migration path is what matters: every interaction should be loggable, every session should carry a device ID, and the code should be written as modules so it can later swap a local prompt for live MEIL context retrieval without being rewritten from scratch.

5. Component Overview and How Each Connects

ComponentRoleConnectionBuild StageNotes
Raspberry Pi Zero 2 WMain Linux compute board. Runs Python, networking, audio tests, and later the voice loop.Boots from microSD. Receives power through micro-USB PWR IN during bare test. Later receives power from PiSugar pogo pins.Stage 1Start here alone. No HAT. No PiSugar. No speaker.
microSD boot cardOperating system and boot media.Inserted into Pi underside slot.Stage 1Uploaded boot partition confirms Raspberry Pi OS boot files and cloud-init style network configuration are present. Keep private; do not publish Wi-Fi details.
5V power adapter / cableKnown-good power for first boot.Plug into the Pi Zero 2 W PWR IN micro-USB port, not the OTG/data port.Stage 1Use only one power source. This prevents a repeat of the prior smoke incident.
ReSpeaker 2-Mics Pi HAT V2.0Microphone array, audio codec, and speaker output.Presses onto the 40-pin GPIO header after shutdown and full power removal.Stage 3Do not attach until bare Pi boot is confirmed. Align every GPIO pin before pressing down.
3W / 4Ω JST speakerSpeech output.JST connector plugs into ReSpeaker HAT speaker output.Stage 4Keep external during bench test. Mount in bear chest only at final enclosure stage.
PiSugar 3 boardPortable battery/UPS power.Mounts beneath Pi using pogo pins against gold contact pads; battery connects to PiSugar via JST.Stage 5Use after Pi and audio HAT are known good. Never power Pi through USB while PiSugar is attached unless the exact safe charging path is confirmed.
PiSugar LiPo batteryPortable power source.JST cable to PiSugar 3.Stage 5Check connector orientation. Do not puncture, crush, or leave loose inside bear.
Temporary clear case / standoffsBench protection and mechanical spacing.Mechanical only; standoffs separate layers.Stage 6Useful for prototype, but not required for first boot.
Teddy bear enclosureFinal soft housing.Speaker mounted toward chest; electronics retained in internal pouch; charging path routed safely.LastBear surgery is last. Do not seal until all electronics and software pass bench tests.

6. First-Morning Build List — Start Here

This is the practical sequence for tomorrow morning. The goal is not to finish the bear. The goal is to establish a known-good Pi, known-good SD card, and known-good network/SSH path. If those pass, the rest of the build becomes orderly. If they fail, attaching audio hardware only adds confusion.

Stage 0 — Workbench setup and safety

  • Clear a non-conductive workspace: wood, cardboard, or anti-static mat. Do not work on metal.
  • Have only these items on the table: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W, imaged microSD card, known-good 5V power cable/adapter, phone/camera for photos, and Windows PC.
  • Keep the ReSpeaker HAT, PiSugar, speaker, standoffs, and bear away from the table for now.
  • Visually inspect the Pi for obvious damage, solder bridges, loose metal, or burnt smell.
  • Confirm the microSD card is fully seated before applying power.

Stage 1 — Bare Pi power-on test

Insert the microSD card into the Pi. Connect power to the Pi Zero 2 W power input only. On the Pi Zero family, one micro-USB port is power and the other is USB OTG/data. Use the power input. Do not connect a keyboard, monitor, ReSpeaker, or PiSugar.

  1. Insert microSD card.
  2. Plug in power.
  3. Watch the small green ACT LED. Some blinking activity is expected during boot.
  4. Wait at least 3–5 minutes for first boot expansion/configuration.
  5. Check your router client list for a new device named sofie-bear or a new Raspberry Pi client.
  6. From Windows, try the SSH checks below.
ping sofie-bear.local
ssh joe@sofie-bear.local

If .local does not resolve, do not assume failure. Check the router for the Pi IP address and try:

ssh joe@<PI_IP_ADDRESS>

Stop immediately if any of this happens

  • Smoke, spark, pop, or burnt smell.
  • Pi gets too hot to touch within the first minute.
  • Power cable or adapter gets hot.
  • ACT LED never blinks and router never sees the device after 5 minutes.

If any stop condition appears, unplug power and do not continue stacking hardware. Photograph the board and pause.

Stage 2 — Once SSH works, run base system checks

These commands only inspect the Pi. They do not install drivers and they do not change the system in a meaningful way.

hostname
hostnamectl
cat /etc/os-release
uname -a
df -h
ip addr
vcgencmd measure_temp

Then shut the Pi down cleanly before touching any hardware:

sudo shutdown now

Wait for LED activity to stop, then remove power. Do not attach or remove HATs while powered.

Stage 3 — ReSpeaker HAT physical attachment, no PiSugar yet

Only after the bare Pi boots and SSH works: with power removed, align the ReSpeaker HAT over the 40-pin header. Inspect from the side to confirm all pins are entering the header. Press down evenly. Keep the speaker disconnected for the first HAT boot if you want the most conservative path.

Boot the Pi again. SSH in. Confirm the system still comes up. Then move to driver setup and audio detection.

aplay -l
arecord -l
lsmod | grep -i tlv

Stage 4 — ReSpeaker driver setup

Use the current Seeed Studio ReSpeaker 2-Mics Pi HAT V2.0 documentation before running driver commands. The current Seeed wiki warns that the old V1 driver method can fail on modern Raspberry Pi OS, including failures where aplay or arecord do not detect the device. Follow the V2.0 path, not old forum snippets.

The current vendor flow is: mount the HAT, confirm the latest Raspberry Pi OS, install/build the TLV320AIC3x I2C driver where required, install the ReSpeaker V2.0 overlay, reboot, then verify with aplay -l and arecord -l.

Stage 5 — Speaker test

After the HAT is visible to aplay and arecord, shut down, unplug power, connect the JST speaker to the HAT, boot again, and test playback at low volume. The speaker should remain outside the bear until the final stage.

alsamixer
speaker-test -t wav -c 2

Stage 6 — PiSugar 3 only after Pi + HAT are proven

The previous smoke event was tied to power architecture risk. Do not introduce PiSugar until the bare Pi and ReSpeaker path are known good. When you do add it, shut down, remove all power, mount PiSugar beneath the Pi, confirm pogo-pin alignment against the underside contact pads, then power using the PiSugar procedure only.

Stage 7 — Software voice loop, after hardware bench tests

Only after hardware works should we create the modular Sofie Bear runtime folder. The code should be built as modules from the start: audio input, wake word, STT, LLM, TTS, audio output, config, and local event logging. This avoids turning the bear into one giant script.

C:\Auri\03_projects\Sofies_Bear\
  README.md
  requirements.txt
  .env.example
  config.yaml
  sopia_prompt.txt
  main.py
  wake_word.py
  audio_input.py
  stt_client.py
  llm_client.py
  tts_client.py
  audio_output.py
  local_event_log.py
  hardware_test.py

7. Current Boot Disk Status

The uploaded SD card archive shows a Raspberry Pi boot partition, including *.dtb, *.elf, kernel8.img, cmdline.txt, config.txt, and an overlays directory. This matches what Windows would normally expose from a Raspberry Pi OS card: the readable boot partition, not the larger Linux root filesystem.

The boot partition also contains Raspberry Pi Imager style metadata and network configuration. That supports the corrected conclusion: the work completed earlier was imaging and configuring the boot disk, not writing the Sofie Bear runtime application.

Privacy note: do not publish the boot card files or screenshots of network configuration. Treat the card as an internal artifact because it may contain network setup details.

8. Software Roadmap

The software should be developed in three passes. Pass one is not AI at all: prove the Pi can record audio and play audio. Pass two is a local/cloud voice loop: record, transcribe, send to Claude with a static Sopia prompt, synthesize speech, and play back. Pass three is the AuriOS integration layer: device ID, local logs, MEIL-ready session format, and later live MQI retrieval.

PassGoalDefinition of Done
Pass 1 — Hardware audioConfirm ReSpeaker capture and speaker playback.arecord records usable audio and aplay or speaker test plays through the JST speaker.
Pass 2 — Standalone voice loopCreate a working bear that talks using a static Sopia persona.Push/test trigger → record → STT → Anthropic response → TTS → speaker output.
Pass 3 — AuriOS-ready nodeMake the bear’s data shape compatible with PAIPy and MEIL.Every interaction has timestamp, device ID, transcript, response, and project tag sopia.
Pass 4 — Future PAIPy nodeReplace static context with live MEIL/MQI retrieval and device registration.Bear authenticates as a registered PAIPy device with defined read/write disclosure level.

9. Risk Log and Guardrails

Hardware guardrails

  • One power source at a time.
  • No hardware changes while powered.
  • Boot bare Pi first.
  • Bench test before bear surgery.
  • Speaker outside the bear until final enclosure.
  • Volume ceiling before infant use.

Software guardrails

  • No API keys committed to source files.
  • Use .env locally and .env.example in project folder.
  • Log sessions locally in JSONL before live MEIL writes.
  • Use original songs only; no copyrighted lyrics.
  • Design for child speech uncertainty and failed transcription.
  • Build modules, not one giant main.py.

10. Source Evidence Inventory

This document is assembled from the recovered project artifacts and current hardware inspection notes. The page should remain an internal working document until secrets, network names, API identifiers, and IP-sensitive language are reviewed.

  • Seeed Studio ReSpeaker V2.0 documentation — current driver setup warning and V2.0 install path.
  • Raspberry Pi official documentation — Imager, headless setup, SSH, Wi-Fi, and boot media process.
